name: Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop
name_original: Talaia d'Alcúdia y Penya des Migdia — circular por la península de Alcúdia
description: Hike the challenging 7.9-mile Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop for panoramic views, historical sites, and diverse terrain.
region_intro: Hiking around Alcúdia offers diverse landscapes, from rugged coastlines to tranquil countryside and dramatic mountain scenery. The region is characterized by limestone peaks, varied terrain, and a mix of coastal paths and shaded pine forests. Notable features include the Alcúdia Peninsula with its highest point, Talaia d'Alcúdia, and the wetlands of S'Albufera Natural Park. Best hiking trails around Alcúdia The most popular hiking route is Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop, a 7.9 miles (12.7 km) trail that takes 4 hours 41 minutes to complete. This difficult route ascends to the highest point on the Alcúdia Peninsula, offering panoramic views. Another top favourite among local hikers is View of Coll Baix beach – View of Platja des Coll Baix loop from puig d'en Vauma, a moderate 5.1 miles (8.2 km) path. This trail leads to a secluded beach, traversing rugged landscapes and coastal cliffs. Local hikers also love the View from Puig de Sant Martí – Lago Esperanza (L'Estany Gran) loop from Protected Area: Estany des Ponts, a 6.7 miles (10.8 km) trail leading through the area around Puig de Sant Martí, often completed in about 3 hours 3 minutes. Hiking around Alcúdia is defined by rugged coastlines, pine forests, and diverse mountain scenery. The network of trails offers options for various ability levels, from easy walks to challenging full-day treks. The routes in Alcúdia are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 7,700 reviews. More than 40,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Alcúdia's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Alcúdia?
A: Alcúdia offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 250 routes available. These trails range from easy, short walks to challenging full-day treks, catering to all fitness levels and preferences.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families in Alcúdia?
A: Yes, Alcúdia has over 120 easy hiking trails perfect for beginners or families. These routes often feature gentle terrain and shorter distances. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31953768" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Platja de Sant Pere – Sa Font de Sant Joan Beach loop from Alcúdia&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a pleasant walk with minimal elevation gain.

Q: What kind of views can I expect on hikes in Alcúdia?
A: Hiking in Alcúdia offers spectacular and diverse views. You can expect panoramic vistas of the Bay of Pollença, Cap Formentor, the northern foothills of the Tramuntana mountains, and the Bay of Alcúdia. Key viewpoints include the summit of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/189797" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talaia d'Alcúdia&lt;/a&gt; and the historic pirate lookout at Penya des Migdia, both offering breathtaking coastal and mountain scenery.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Alcúdia?
A: Mallorca's mild climate makes Alcúdia a year-round hiking destination. However, spring and autumn are particularly favored for their comfortable temperatures, vibrant landscapes, and pleasant weather conditions, making them ideal for exploring the trails.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Alcúdia?
A: Yes, many of Alcúdia's trails are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999146518/talaia-d-alcudia-and-penya-des-migdia-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through diverse terrain and offers stunning views before returning to your starting point.

Q: Can I find trails that lead to secluded beaches or natural attractions?
A: Absolutely. Alcúdia is known for trails leading to beautiful, secluded spots. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/35966611" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Coll Baix beach – View of Platja des Coll Baix loop from puig d'en Vauma&lt;/a&gt; is a great example, traversing rugged landscapes and coastal cliffs to reach the stunning Platja des Coll Baix.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest along the hiking trails?
A: Yes, Alcúdia's rich history is woven into its landscapes. You can explore the ancient Roman walls of Alcúdia's Old Town or visit the Ermita de la Victoria, an old monastery that serves as a starting point for several hikes on the Alcúdia Peninsula. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/189797" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talaia d'Alcúdia — Summit and Ruins&lt;/a&gt; highlight also points to historical remnants at the peninsula's highest point.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Alcúdia?
A: The hiking trails in Alcúdia are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 7,700 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse scenery, from rugged coastlines and pine forests to dramatic mountain views, as well as the variety of trails suitable for all skill levels.

Q: Are there opportunities for wildlife spotting or nature observation on the trails?
A: Yes, particularly in areas like S'Albufera Natural Park, which is the most important wetland area in the Balearic Islands. This protected area offers peaceful nature trails perfect for birdwatching and exploring diverse flora, providing a different kind of natural beauty compared to the coastal and mountain routes.

Q: Are there any hikes that offer views of the Mallorcan plain?
A: For views of the Mallorcan plain, consider trails around Puig de Sant Martí. The southern peak of Puig de Sant Martí provides extensive views of the plain, in addition to panoramas of the Alcúdia district, its town, port, and both the Bay of Alcúdia and Pollença. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24546906" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View from Puig de Sant Martí – Lago Esperanza (L'Estany Gran) loop from Protected Area: Estany des Ponts&lt;/a&gt; is a great way to explore this area.

Q: Is it possible to hike along the dramatic Cap de Formentor coastline?
A: Yes, the Cap de Formentor offers dramatic and scenic trails along a rugged coastline. This area is renowned for its breathtaking views and culminates in the striking Formentor lighthouse. While not directly in Alcúdia, it's a prominent feature of the northern coast and accessible for a memorable hiking experience.

Q: Are there any trails that start near the old monastery of Ermita de la Victoria?
A: The Ermita de la Victoria is a popular starting point for several circular hikes on the Alcúdia Peninsula. These trails offer a gradual ascent with spectacular coastal views of the Bay of Pollensa, Cape Formentor, and on clear days, even Menorca.
tips: At the crossroads where you can descend to the beach Platja des Col Baix, there is a well-kept rest area with shelter and a simple toilet. The wooden benches stand in the shade of large pine trees and you have a beautiful view of the bay with the steep cliffs.

Accessible via several routes. Coming from the south, it's much more scenic, but also more strenuous.

The tin hut is often occupied and serves as a fire watchtower.

Great views over the Badia de Pollença, Puig Romaní, Cap Formentor, the Penya Roja, the northern foothills of the Tramuntana, the Badia d'Alcúdia and its resorts, and even Cap de Ferrutx.


If approaching from the south, it should be easier to find the way forward from the summit.

Coming from the Ermita de la Victoria, a wide and comfortable path leads up to the summit of the Talaia d'Alcúdia. As soon as the slope becomes steeper, the path winds its way along the rock face in narrow serpentines. Even if the road is not secured, there is no danger here. But you should still be free from dizziness.

Great view to the Cap Formentor, over the Cap Pinar and the Puig Romaní.
The last piece is a bit steep and screeching.

faqs.question: How long does it take to hike the Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop?
faqs.answer: This challenging loop typically takes around 4 hours and 40 minutes to complete. However, allow extra time for enjoying the panoramic views and exploring the historical sites along the way.

faqs.question: Where does the Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop trail start?
faqs.answer: The hike often begins from the 13th-century Ermita de la Victoria, a cultural highlight itself. There is parking available near this hermitage, making it a convenient starting point for the loop.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this hike?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied and demanding. You'll encounter mountain paths, gravel roads, and natural, unpaved trails. Expect rocky, limestone, and karst landscapes, with sections through shaded pine forests, garrigue scrubland, and narrow gorges. Some parts are winding with narrow sections and switchbacks, and can involve dry stream beds. Be prepared for uneven footing.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop?
faqs.answer: This route offers breathtaking 360-degree panoramic views of Pollença Bay, Alcúdia Bay, the Formentor peninsula, and the Tramuntana mountains. Notable features include the summit of Talaia d'Alcúdia — Summit and Ruins, the Penya des Migdia Summit and Cannon Viewpoint with its 17th-century bronze cannon, and the remote View of Platja des Coll Baix. You'll also pass the Coll Baix Refuge, which offers a shaded rest area.

faqs.question: Is the Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is considered difficult and is not suitable for beginners. It requires a good level of fitness, sure-footedness, and sturdy hiking shoes. Some sections are technical, difficult, or even hazardous, and may necessitate prior alpine experience and potentially specialist equipment. The final ascent to Penya des Migdia includes steep climbs that may require using your hands.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best times to hike are typically spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Avoid very windy days, as some sections along cliff edges can be particularly challenging and exposed. Summers can be very hot, making the strenuous climb more difficult.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop?
faqs.answer: While there are no explicit restrictions mentioned, due to the trail's difficult and technical nature, with narrow and exposed sections, it may not be suitable for all dogs. If you bring your dog, ensure they are fit, well-behaved, and comfortable with challenging terrain, and always keep them on a leash.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this hike?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to hike the Talaia d'Alcúdia and Penya des Migdia Loop.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty and varied terrain, you should bring sturdy hiking shoes with good grip, plenty of water, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), snacks, and a map or GPS device. Trekking poles can be helpful for stability on the rocky and gravelly sections. For the more technical parts, prior alpine experience and potentially specialist equipment might be necessary.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the camí de la Talaia, comellar de ses Fontanelles Sobiranes, and camí de la Penya des Migdia.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenging sections or insider tips for this route?
faqs.answer: Be prepared for the final ascent to Penya des Migdia, which involves steep climbs where you might need to use your hands and follow painted markers on the rock. The descent can also be demanding, with gravelly, trackless terrain. Some sections are exposed along cliff edges, which can be challenging on windy days. Always prioritize sure-footedness and proper gear.

Schöner Weg unterhalb der Talaia d'Alcúdia
